summary: Gruber responds to Walt Mossberg's column asking why Siri seems so dumb, agreeing with the core criticism while pinpointing Siri's fundamental weakness: its inability to maintain context across sequential commands. He demonstrates this with transcribed examples where Siri correctly answers a question about the next presidential debate but then cannot use that information to create a calendar event or reminder. Gruber argues that the bigger problem isn't just Siri's current limitations but the credibility damage — users stop trying after being burned too many times. He notes, however, that Google Assistant fails similarly at chaining commands, suggesting Siri may not be as far behind the competition as perceived.
key_points: Siri's core weakness is its inability to maintain conversational context and chain together sequential commands
Glaring inconsistencies across Apple devices (Siri working on iPhone but failing on Mac/iPad) are almost as damaging as universal failures
Apple faces a 'boy who cried wolf' credibility problem where users have stopped trying Siri even if it improves
Google Assistant fails at the same chaining tasks, suggesting Siri may not actually be far behind the competition
Apple fixed specific failures Mossberg tweeted about, but the systemic context problem remains
money_quotes: Where Siri fundamentally falls apart is its inability to maintain context and chain together multiple commands.
These sort of glaring inconsistencies are almost as bad as universal failures.
The big problem Apple faces with Siri is that when people encounter these problems, they stop trying.
It feels like you're wasting your time, and makes you feel silly or even foolish for having tried.
I worry that even if Apple improves Siri significantly, people will never know it because they won't bother trying because they were burned so many times before.
In addition to the engineering hurdles to actually make Siri much better, Apple also has to overcome a 'boy who cried wolf' credibility problem.
I can't imagine how stupid an actual human assistant would have to be not to understand a request like 'Find out when the next debate is and put it on my calendar.'
I remain unconvinced that Siri is behind the competition, and even if it is, I don't think it's by much.
key_insight: Siri's inability to chain contextual commands is a fundamental architectural failure, but the deeper threat to Apple is the credibility damage that makes users stop trying even if Siri improves.
